hero.tagline: Arkansas small claims courts offer a streamlined process for disputes up to $5,000, with attorneys not permitted to represent parties.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Trial de novo in Circuit Court

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If a claim exceeds the $5,000 cap, you must either waive the excess amount or file in a higher court. Splitting one dispute into multiple claims to fit under the cap is not permitted.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Arkansas allows you to file claims for breaches of contract and money owed up to $5,000. The statute of limitations varies depending on the type of contract.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend someone $2,000, and they failed to repay.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Gather any evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned a friend $1,500 with the promise of repayment in six months, but they haven't paid.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the loan agreement and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $3,000 and failed to make payments as agreed.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided $4,500 worth of consulting services, but the client hasn't paid the invoice.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Bring the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $2,000 on their account with your business.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $3,500 in unpaid rent over several months.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Present the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid with a $1,200 check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: 30-day notice required before filing. Provide the bounced check and notice of dishonor.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $4,000 loan, and the primary borrower defaulted, leaving you responsible.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Bring the loan agreement and records of payments made on behalf of the borrower.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Arkansas small claims courts handle various consumer disputes up to $5,000, including issues with products and services.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,000 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to refund or replace it.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from purchase. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged $800 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement and evidence of inadequate service.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,500 for repairs that did not fix the issue, and refuses to refund.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from repair date. Bring repair invoices and any expert opinions on the faulty repair.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory work by a contractor
claims.example: A contractor was paid $4,000 for home renovations but left the work incomplete.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from contract date. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You paid a DJ $1,200 for an event, but they never showed up and refused to refund.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from event date. Bring the service agreement and proof of payment.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month gym membership, but they continued to charge your account.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from first unauthorized charge. Provide cancellation confirmation and bank statements.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $4,500 that broke down within a week, and the seller refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from purchase. Bring the bill of sale and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A company advertised a product for $200 with features it doesn't have, and refuses to refund.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from purchase. Provide the advertisement and proof of purchase.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Arkansas small claims courts address property damage claims up to $5,000, including vehicle collisions and damage to personal property.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver caused $3,000 in damage to your car and refuses to pay for repairs.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from accident date. Bring the police report, repair estimates, and photos of the damage.

claims.name: Neighbor caused damage to your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell on your fence, causing $2,500 in damage, and they refuse to compensate.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos of the damage and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left $4,000 worth of damage upon moving out, beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery of damage. Bring the lease agreement, photos of the damage, and repair estimates.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog damaged your $1,500 garden, and they refuse to pay for repairs.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos of the damage and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left a $2,000 watch with a jeweler for repair, and they damaged it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery of damage. Bring the service agreement and photos of the damage.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Arkansas small claims courts handle monetary disputes between landlords and tenants up to $5,000, excluding eviction proceedings.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,200 security deposit without justification.
claims.notes: 60-day deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement and any communication regarding the deposit.

claims.name: Landlord failed to provide itemized deductions from deposit
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $800 from your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 60-day deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease agreement and any communication regarding the deposit.

claims.name: Landlord failed to maintain habitable conditions
claims.example: Your rental had no heating during winter, and the landlord refused to fix it.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from issue occurrence. Provide photos and records of communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord wrongfully retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, the landlord kept your $2,000 worth of personal belongings.
claims.notes: 60-day deadline from lease termination. Provide an inventory of the items and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your rental.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from eviction date. Provide evidence of the eviction and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord failed to return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: You paid the last month's rent in advance, but the landlord didn't return it after you moved out early.
claims.notes: 60-day deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement and proof of prepaid rent.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant owes $3,000 in unpaid rent over several months.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Provide the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent without landlord consent
claims.example: You repaired a broken heater for $500 and deducted it from rent without landlord approval.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from repair date. Provide repair receipts and communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Arkansas small claims courts handle disputes over unpaid wages and related issues up to $5,000.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 in unpaid wages for hours worked.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from wage due date. Provide pay stubs and records of hours worked.

claims.name: Employer failed to provide final paycheck
claims.example: After termination, your employer didn't give you the final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from termination date. Provide termination notice and records of unpaid wages.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in sales commissions that your employer hasn't paid.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from commission due date. Provide sales records and commission agreements.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 20 hours of overtime but weren't paid the additional $600 owed.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from overtime worked. Provide timesheets and pay records.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Arkansas small claims courts address personal disputes up to $5,000, including minor injuries and property issues.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a neighbor's icy sidewalk, resulting in $2,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from injury date. Provide medical records and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You loaned a friend your $1,500 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from demand for return. Provide evidence of the loan and requests for return.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A roommate took your $2,000 laptop without permission and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from theft date. Provide police reports and evidence of ownership.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Arkansas small claims courts handle vehicle-related disputes up to $5,000, including repair issues and wrongful towing.

claims.name: Dispute over vehicle repairs
claims.example: A mechanic charged $3,000 for repairs that didn't fix the problem, and refuses to refund.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from repair date. Provide repair invoices and any expert opinions on the faulty repair.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from your apartment complex without proper notice, resulting in $500 in fees.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from towing date. Provide towing receipts and any communication with the towing company.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction cases are not handled in small claims court and must be filed in the civil division of the District Court.
Disputes involving the ownership of real property are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Claims involving defamation, such as libel or slander, are not suitable for small claims court due to their complexity.
Professional malpractice claims require expert testimony and are not appropriate for small claims court.
Issues such as divorce, child custody, and support are handled by family courts, not small claims court.
Disputes involving wills, estates, and trusts are under the jurisdiction of probate courts.
Small claims courts do not have jurisdiction over cases against federal agencies.
Class action lawsuits are complex and exceed the procedural scope of small claims court.
Small claims courts cannot issue orders requiring a party to do or refrain from doing something.
Disputes over workers' compensation benefits are handled by the Workers' Compensation Commission.
Bankruptcy cases are under the exclusive jurisdiction of federal bankruptcy courts.
Disputes subject to mandatory arbitration clauses are not suitable for small claims court.
Claims against deceased individuals must be filed against their estate in probate court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases, such as bad check claims.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees are generally not recoverable in small claims court, as attorneys are not permitted to represent parties.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims Division of the District Court

filing_options: Any county where one defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: Availability of e-filing depends on the specific District Court; check with the local court clerk.

service_fees.notes: Fees vary by county; contact local sheriff's office for rates.
Fees vary; contact process servers for quotes.

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and may cover service fees; check with the court clerk.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees may be added to the judgment against the defendant.

other_fees.name: Subpoena issuance
other_fees.notes: Contact the court clerk for current fees.

other_fees.name: Appeal filing
other_fees.notes: Fees vary by county; check with the Circuit Court clerk.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are typically added to the judgment against the defendant.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge

entries.notes: Discovery rule applies; clock starts when fraud is discovered or should have been discovered.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims; the statute of limitations begins when the fraud is discovered or should have been discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy stays. Partial payment can restart the clock.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention. For bad check claims, a 30-day notice is required before filing.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: Before filing a claim for a bad check, you must send a written demand for payment, allowing the issuer 30 days to respond.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific pre-filing demand required for wage claims, but it's advisable to request payment in writing before filing.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: For security deposit disputes, provide the landlord with a forwarding address in writing to facilitate the return of the deposit.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No specific pre-filing requirements for consumer protection claims, but documenting all communications is beneficial.

forms.name: Statement of Claim
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's allegations and the relief sought.

forms.name: Answer
forms.description: Allows the defendant to respond to the plaintiff's Statement of Claim.

forms.name: Counterclaim
forms.description: Filed by the defendant if they have a claim against the plaintiff.

forms.name: Satisfaction of Judgment
forms.description: Filed by the plaintiff to indicate that the judgment has been satisfied.

forms.name: Affidavit of Indigency
forms.description: Used to request a waiver of court fees due to financial hardship.

methods.name: Certified mail
methods.description: The court clerk sends the documents to the defendant via certified mail with return receipt requested.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: A sheriff or constable personally delivers the documents to the defendant.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A licensed individual delivers the documents to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent for corporations; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Arkansas rules and the laws of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, you may request permission from the court for alternative service methods, such as publication.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document your attempts and request alternative service methods from the court.

response_and_default.response_notes: The defendant must file a written answer within 30 days of being served to avoid default judgment.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to respond or appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim before the court awards judgment.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the defendant may appeal the decision within 30 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If a counterclaim exceeds the small claims limit, the case may be transferred to the civil division of the District Court.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Attorneys are not permitted to represent parties in small claims court; parties must represent themselves.
hearing.format: Bench trial with informal procedures; each party presents their case directly to the judge.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Contact the court clerk in advance to request an interpreter; fees may apply.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some courts may offer mediation services; check with the local court clerk.

appeals.type_notes: The appeal is a new trial in the Circuit Court, where both parties can present their cases anew.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed within 30 days; the appeal will result in a new trial in Circuit Court.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of the debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer will withhold a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when the debtor is employed and earns above the minimum threshold for garnishment.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of garnishment, serve it on the debtor's bank, and the bank will freeze and remit funds to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the bank account; may require knowledge of the debtor's banking information.
exemptions: Social Security and federal benefits are exempt

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property to secure the judgment amount.
methods.description: File a certified copy of the judgment with the county recorder's office to place a lien on the debtor's property; the lien must be satisfied before the property can be sold or refinanced.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property; may take time to realize payment, as the lien is typically satisfied upon sale or refinancing.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt personal property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, and the sheriff will seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt property at public auction to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt personal property; may be time-consuming and yield limited proceeds.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before the 10-year period expires; renewal extends the judgment for an additional 10 years.

debtor_exam_form.name: Order for Examination of Judgment Debtor

collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged in bankruptcy.
collection.priority_notes: Judgment liens are prioritized based on the date of recording; earlier liens have higher priority.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can represent themselves in small claims court; however, attorneys are not permitted to represent any party.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a fictitious name (DBA) must file a Certificate of Assumed Name with the county clerk.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may authorize an employee to represent them in small claims court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face challenges in enforcing contracts in court; ensure proper licensing before filing a claim.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: Suing the wrong party or using incorrect names can result in dismissal or unenforceable judgments.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the exact legal name and address of the defendant before filing; for businesses, check the Secretary of State's records.

pitfalls.title: Exceeding the monetary limit
pitfalls.what_happens: Claims exceeding $5,000 are not permitted in small claims court and may be dismissed.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Ensure your claim, excluding interest and court costs, does not exceed the $5,000 limit.

pitfalls.title: Missing the statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will result in dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Determine the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type and file within that period.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to properly serve the defendant can delay proceedings or lead to dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow the court's rules for service of process carefully, using approved methods and documenting service.

pitfalls.title: Lack of evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Insufficient evidence can result in losing your case, even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements to support your claim.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant files a counterclaim and you are unprepared, you could lose and owe money.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Be prepared to defend against potential counterclaims by reviewing the defendant's answer and gathering necessary evidence.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, attorneys are not permitted to represent parties in Arkansas small claims court; individuals and businesses must represent themselves.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The typical timeline from filing to hearing is between 30 to 90 days, depending on the court's schedule and case complexity.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can sue for in Arkansas small claims court is $5,000, excluding interest and court costs.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in your favor; however, you must still present evidence to support your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims court decision by filing a notice of appeal within 30 days; the appeal will result in a new trial in Circuit Court.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Arkansas is valid for 10 years and can be renewed before expiration to extend its enforceability.

state_specific_notes: Arkansas small claims courts do not allow attorneys to represent parties; individuals and businesses must represent themselves.
The maximum claim amount in Arkansas small claims court is $5,000, excluding interest and court costs.
Eviction cases are not handled in small claims court and must be filed in the civil division of the District Court.
A 30-day written demand is required before filing a claim for a bad check.
Judgments are valid for 10 years and can be renewed before expiration to extend enforceability.

interesting_findings: Arkansas small claims courts prohibit attorney representation to simplify proceedings and reduce costs.
The state requires a 30-day written demand before pursuing legal action for bad checks, providing an opportunity for resolution without court intervention.

sources.label: Ark. Code Ann. § 16-17-601 - Small Claims Division - Jurisdiction
Ark. Code Ann. § 4-88-107 - Deceptive Trade Practices Act (UDAP)
Ark. Code Ann. § 5-37-307 - Civil Penalty for Bad Checks
Ark. Code Ann. § 18-16-303 - Security Deposit Return
Ark. Code Ann. § 11-4-405 - Final Paycheck Deadline
